compiled_state.title: Session discards unmerged changes on merge conflict without verifying work is not lost — assumes conflicts mean work was duplicated
compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart
compiled_state.description:
## What happened (P1)

A session encountered merge conflicts when running merge-to-main.sh and concluded that the conflicts meant the work was already done on main. Instead of resolving the conflicts or presenting them to the user, the session recommended discarding the worktree changes.

## Why this is dangerous

Merge conflicts do NOT mean the work was duplicated. Common causes of merge conflicts include:
- Another session modified the same files for different reasons
- Upstream refactoring changed the same code areas
- Config file changes that overlap structurally but have different content
- Test file additions that conflict on import ordering

In all these cases, the worktree's changes are NOT present on main and discarding them loses work.

## Expected behavior

When merge-to-main.sh encounters conflicts:
1. The session should analyze each conflicted file to determine whether the worktree's changes are already present on main
2. If ANY worktree change is NOT present on main, the session MUST either resolve the conflict or escalate to the user
3. The session should NEVER recommend discarding a worktree with unmerged commits without confirming that all changes are present on main

## Fix needed

1. Add a conflict analysis step that checks whether worktree changes are already on main (git diff main..HEAD for each conflicted file)
2. Block the 'discard worktree' recommendation unless all worktree changes are confirmed as present on main
3. Default to escalating conflicts to the user rather than discarding

comments.body: INTERACTIVITY_DEFERRED: safeguard_approval | Behavioral: session discards changes on merge conflict. Fix requires editing scripts/merge-to-main.sh or skills (safeguard). Re-run in interactive session to approve.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart
